vulnerabilities.title: No Minimum Owner Count Allows Permanent Wallet Bricking via Owner Removal
vulnerabilities.summary: removeOwnerAtIndex has no check to ensure at least one owner remains. A single owner (or the last remaining owner) can remove ALL owners including themselves. With zero owners, no one can call any onlyOwner function (addOwner, execute, upgrade), permanently locking all funds with no recovery mechanism.

description.desc: removeOwnerAtIndex deletes owner without checking remaining owner count; no activeOwnerCount state variable exists

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - Permanent, irrecoverable loss of all wallet funds. No on-chain recovery mechanism exists: initialize() is blocked by the Initialized guard, all owner management functions require onlyOwner which permanently fails, and the EntryPoint validation path fails because no valid owner exists for signature verification. Any ETH, ERC-20 tokens, or NFTs held by the wallet become permanently inaccessible.

vulnerabilities.title: WebAuthn authenticatorData Minimum Length Not Validated Before Flag Access
vulnerabilities.summary: WebAuthn.verify accesses webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] to check UP and UV flags without first verifying that authenticatorData has at least 33 bytes. If authenticatorData is shorter than 33 bytes, the access causes an out-of-bounds revert rather than a graceful false return. This forces _validateSignature to revert instead of returning false, which has different semantic meaning in ERC-4337 validation.

description.desc: webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] accessed without length >= 33 check; out-of-bounds revert instead of returning false

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a code defect where invalid WebAuthn signatures cause reverts instead of graceful failures. In ERC-4337 context, this changes the semantic from 'invalid signature' (return 1) to 'validation error' (revert). Bundlers that simulate UserOps off-chain would detect the revert during simulation and reject the UserOp, so on-chain gas waste is limited. For ERC-1271, callers expecting either 0x1626ba7e or 0xffffffff would instead receive a revert, potentially breaking integrating protocols. This is a defensive programming defect - all other validation paths in verify() return false, but this one reverts.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicSpend Paymaster Batch Balance Over-Commitment Leading to Solvency Violation
vulnerabilities.summary: In validatePaymasterUserOp, the balance check `address(this).balance < withdrawAmount` is performed independently per UserOperation. When a bundler includes multiple UserOps from the same or different senders using MagicSpend as paymaster, each validation independently passes the balance check against the same contract balance (which hasn't changed during validation). The cumulative _withdrawableETH can exceed the contract's actual ETH holdings. During postOp execution, the first postOp drains a

description.desc: address(this).balance check is per-UserOp; _withdrawableETH[userOp.sender] += withdrawAmount - maxCost is additive across ops in same bundle

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. The later UserOp in the bundle loses its gas refund (maxGasCost - actualGasCost), and the assert failure in postOp consumes all remaining gas. The _withdrawableETH for the affected user persists (rolled back delete), so they can later recover withdrawAmount-maxCost via withdrawGasExcess() if the contract is refunded. However, the gas refund difference is permanently lost. In extreme cases, this could be weaponized by a malicious bundler to grief specific users. The contract's solvency invariant (sum of _withdrawableETH <= balance) is violated.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain UUPS Upgrade Replay via executeWithoutChainIdValidation
vulnerabilities.summary: The canSkipChainIdValidation function returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, allowing upgrade UserOperations to be replayed across all chains where the wallet exists at the same address. An attacker observes a legitimate upgrade on chain A (from on-chain data), then replays the identical UserOperation on chain B. If the implementation address contains different code on chain B (e.g., malicious contract deployed at same address via CREATE2, or no code at all), the wallet is c

description.desc: canSkipChainIdValidation returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, enabling cross-chain replay of implementation upgrades

vulnerabilities.impact:
CRITICAL. Successful exploitation results in complete wallet takeover on the target chain:
- All ETH and tokens in the wallet are at risk
- Attacker gains permanent control via malicious implementation
- Affects all CoinbaseSmartWallet instances deployed at same address across chains
- The defect is in the core security design - whitelisting a privilege-escalation function (upgradeToAndCall) for cross-chain replay without any mechanism to verify implementation code consistency

vulnerabilities.title: Uninitialized Proxy Takeover via Empty Owners Array in initialize()
vulnerabilities.summary: CoinbaseSmartWallet.initialize() checks nextOwnerIndex() != 0 as its only re-initialization guard. If initialize is called with an empty owners array, _initializeOwners loops zero times, nextOwnerIndex remains 0, and the wallet remains re-initializable. Any subsequent caller can invoke initialize() with their own owner set. The factory enforces owners.length > 0, but the wallet's initialize() does not. Any proxy deployed outside the factory (manual ERC1967 proxy deployment) is vulnerable.

description.desc: initialize() checks nextOwnerIndex() != 0 but calling with empty array leaves nextOwnerIndex at 0, allowing re-call
Affected function: _initializeOwners

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL for non-factory-deployed proxies. An attacker gains complete control of the wallet, including: (1) executing arbitrary calls to drain all ETH and tokens, (2) upgrading the implementation to a malicious contract, (3) adding/removing owners at will. For factory-deployed wallets, the factory's owners.length check at line 44 mitigates this. The defect is in the wallet contract's defense-in-depth — it relies entirely on external callers to pass non-empty owner arrays.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicSpend postOp Assert Failure Creates Unrecoverable Fund Loss
vulnerabilities.summary: MagicSpend.postOp asserts `mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted`. If the initial postOp call fails (e.g., forceSafeTransferETH fails due to insufficient contract balance from batch over-commitment in ECON-H001, or extreme gas conditions), the EntryPoint re-invokes postOp with PostOpMode.postOpReverted. The assert statement consumes all remaining gas, and the user's _withdrawableETH was already deleted in the first (reverted) postOp call, but the revert restored it. However, the UserOp's gas cost is

description.desc: assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) creates hard failure; no graceful recovery path for failed ETH transfers

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect's actual impact is DoS, not fund loss. When triggered: (1) In canonical EntryPoint v0.6 where the second postOp revert is not caught: the entire handleOps reverts, no state changes persist, no funds are lost, but the bundler's gas is wasted. (2) If an EntryPoint implementation catches the second revert: the user loses only the gas refund (maxCost - actualGasCost) but can recover their withdrawal via withdrawGasExcess(). The assert also violates the ERC-4337 specification, which requires postOp with postOpReverted mode to not revert.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain Wallet Bricking via Upgrade Replay to Non-Existent Implementation
vulnerabilities.summary: The canSkipChainIdValidation function whitelists upgradeToAndCall.selector for cross-chain replay. A UserOp performing a UUPS upgrade via executeWithoutChainIdValidation is signed without chain ID binding. After an upgrade is executed on chain A, anyone can observe the on-chain transaction and replay the identical UserOp on chain B. If the new implementation address does not have deployed code on chain B (common when deployments are staggered across chains), the wallet proxy points to empty code

description.desc: canSkipChainIdValidation returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, enabling cross-chain replay of implementation upgrades

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. If the implementation has no code on chain B, the wallet proxy delegates to empty code, making all functions return empty data. The wallet cannot execute transactions, cannot upgrade further, and cannot recover. All ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs held by the wallet on chain B are permanently locked with no recovery path short of a chain hard fork. If an attacker deploys malicious code at the implementation address on chain B via CREATE2, they gain full control of the wallet and can drain all assets.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicSpend Paymaster Violates ERC-7562 Storage Access Rules, Causing Bundler Rejection
vulnerabilities.summary: The `validatePaymasterUserOp` function checks `address(this).balance` to ensure sufficient funds. However, ERC-7562 strictly prohibits accessing the paymaster's own balance during the validation phase to prevent unpredictable simulation results. Standard ERC-4337 bundlers will automatically reject any UserOp utilizing this paymaster, causing a complete denial of service on public mempools.

description.desc: if (address(this).balance < withdrawAmount) { revert InsufficientBalance(...); }

vulnerabilities.impact: If used with standard ERC-4337 bundlers enforcing ERC-7562, the MagicSpend paymaster is completely non-functional — 100% of UserOps using it would be rejected during simulation. This constitutes a complete denial of service for users relying on public mempool infrastructure. The financial impact is indirect: users cannot use the sponsored transaction functionality. The fix requires either (a) replacing the balance check with internal accounting via a storage variable tracking available liquidity, or (b) ensuring all users route through a private bundler that bypasses this restriction. Note that some bundlers may not enforce this rule strictly, reducing the practical impact.

vulnerabilities.title: MagicSpend cumulative solvency break: multiple validations can over-credit _withdrawableETH beyond available ETH, enabling later drains of future deposits
vulnerabilities.summary: Because validatePaymasterUserOp credits _withdrawableETH per UserOp after only a per-UserOp balance check (address(this).balance >= withdrawAmount), an attacker can submit multiple UserOps (same or different senders) that each individually pass the balance check but collectively exceed MagicSpend's ETH. If EntryPoint does not fully revert paymaster state for later failures, this can leave unbacked _withdrawableETH that can later be withdrawn when the paymaster receives new ETH.

description.desc: per-op balance check without reserving cumulative commitments; additive credit to withdrawable mapping

vulnerabilities.impact: The solvency invariant (sum of _withdrawableETH <= address(this).balance) can be violated. Unbacked _withdrawableETH credits persist after failed postOp calls and can be realized when the paymaster is replenished. This enables extraction of ETH that belongs to the paymaster operator or is intended for other users. The financial impact scales with the number of signed WithdrawRequests an attacker can obtain and the paymaster's balance.

vulnerabilities.title: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve Accepts Non-Canonical P-256 Points Enabling Persistent Owner Backdoor
vulnerabilities.summary: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve does not reject coordinates x > p or y > p (except exact equality x == p or y == p). Since all arithmetic uses mulmod with p, a point (x+p, y) is mathematically identical to (x, y) but encodes to different bytes via abi.encode. MultiOwnable.addOwnerPublicKey stores the raw bytes without canonicalization, and _addOwnerAtIndex only checks exact byte equality for deduplication (AlreadyOwner). An existing owner can register equivalent non-canonical public keys as separate owners.

description.desc: ecAff_isOnCurve rejects x==p and y==p but NOT x>p or y>p; mulmod(x,x,p) reduces x mod p, so non-canonical coordinates pass the curve check
Affected function: addOwnerPublicKey(bytes32,bytes32)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: A malicious owner can create an irremovable backdoor by registering an algebraically equivalent but bytewise-distinct public key. This undermines the entire owner removal mechanism. After apparent removal, the attacker retains full wallet control including: executing arbitrary calls (draining all funds), adding/removing owners, upgrading the implementation. The impact extends to all assets held by the wallet and all co-owners' trust in the ownership model.

vulnerabilities.title: Selective Cross-Chain Replay of Owner Management Enables Permanent Unauthorized Access
vulnerabilities.summary: The `executeWithoutChainIdValidation` function allows replaying owner additions and removals across chains. Because replays are permissionless and optional, an attacker can observe a sequence of user actions (e.g., Add Owner X, then Remove Owner X) on Chain A, and selectively replay ONLY the 'Add Owner X' operation on Chain B, granting Owner X permanent unauthorized access on Chain B.

description.desc: Whitelisting `addOwnerAddress` and `removeOwnerAtIndex` for chain-agnostic replay without sequence enforcement

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL — If the replayed owner is controlled by or accessible to the attacker, they gain full wallet control on the target chain. This includes: executing arbitrary transactions (draining all ETH, tokens, NFTs), adding/removing owners (locking out the legitimate user), upgrading the wallet implementation (permanent takeover). The attack window is indefinite — the replayable UserOp remains valid on any chain where the nonce sequence hasn't been consumed.

vulnerabilities.title: RIP-7212 Precompile False Positive on Chains with Arbitrary Code at Address 0x100
vulnerabilities.summary: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (VERIFIER) for RIP-7212 precompile signature verification. If no code exists at 0x100, staticcall returns success=true with empty returndata, and the code falls through to FCL (safe). However, on target chains where a regular contract exists at address 0x100 (any of: Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis), the staticcall may return arbitrary data. If ret.length > 0 and abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1, the function returns true immediatel

description.desc: staticcall to hardcoded VERIFIER=0x100 with only ret.length>0 check; no validation that 0x100 is actually RIP-7212 precompile vs arbitrary contract

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploitable on any target chain, this enables complete wallet takeover for ALL wallets using passkey owners on that chain. The attacker can: drain all ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs; add their own owner address; remove all existing owners; and upgrade the implementation to a malicious contract. Recovery is impossible once the attacker controls the wallet. The severity is CRITICAL but conditional on chain-specific state at 0x100.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Owner Removal Enables Multi-Chain Wallet Bricking
vulnerabilities.summary: removeOwnerAtIndex is whitelisted in canSkipChainIdValidation. A signed owner removal UserOp is replayable across all chains. Combined with the lack of minimum owner count (TECH-H003), a single signed UserOp removing the last owner can be replayed to brick the wallet on ALL chains simultaneously. The attacker only needs to observe one chain's removal operation.

description.desc: removeOwnerAtIndex.selector returns true from canSkipChainIdValidation; combined with no minimum owner count in MultiOwnable
Affected function: removeOwnerAtIndex(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: **Severity: CRITICAL**. Permanent, irrecoverable loss of ALL wallet funds on ALL affected chains. The zero-owner state cannot be reversed because: (1) addOwnerAddress/addOwnerPublicKey require onlyOwner, (2) execute/executeBatch require onlyEntryPointOrOwner, (3) _validateSignature requires a registered owner for signature verification, (4) upgradeToAndCall requires onlyOwner. No recovery mechanism exists. This affects any cross-chain wallet user performing owner management operations.

vulnerabilities.title: WebAuthn Signature Forgery via Spoofed RIP-7212 Precompile on Non-Supporting Chains
vulnerabilities.summary: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (the RIP-7212 precompile) before falling back to FCL. The check only verifies `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1`. On chains where a regular contract is deployed at address 0x100 (not the legitimate precompile), if that contract returns 32 bytes containing uint256(1) for arbitrary inputs, ALL passkey signature verifications would pass without actual cryptographic validation. This would allow any attacker to impersonate any

description.desc: staticcall to VERIFIER (0x100) with only length check `ret.length > 0` and decode check `== 1`, no validation that response is from legitimate precompile
Affected function: _validateSignature

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL if exploitable on a target chain. Complete bypass of passkey signature verification enables: wallet takeover, arbitrary fund transfers, owner manipulation, and implementation upgrades. All wallets using passkey owners on the affected chain would be vulnerable. However, exploitation probability is limited by the environmental requirement of a specific contract at 0x100.

vulnerabilities.title: Physical Access Attack via Missing User Verification in WebAuthn
vulnerabilities.summary: The SmartWallet hardcodes `requireUV: false` when validating WebAuthn signatures. This disables the requirement for User Verification (biometrics/PIN). An attacker with physical access to an unlocked device can authorize arbitrary transactions by simply tapping the security key or clicking accept, bypassing the intended multi-factor security of passkeys.

description.desc: WebAuthn.verify({..., requireUV: false, ...})
Affected function: verify

vulnerabilities.impact: This is a design-level security policy choice, not a code defect. The impact is that the contract does not reject signatures where the authenticator did not perform user verification. In practice, most modern passkey authenticators (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager) perform UV by default regardless of the RP's request. The Phase A analysis itself categorized this as 'low' exploitation potential. The contract correctly enforces User Presence (UP), which requires physical interaction with the authenticator.

vulnerabilities.title: CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory.createAccount Permanently Locks ETH for Already-Deployed Accounts
vulnerabilities.summary: When createAccount is called with msg.value > 0 for an already-deployed wallet (alreadyDeployed == true from LibClone.createDeterministicERC1967), the CREATE2 instruction fails silently because the address is occupied. The msg.value ETH is NOT sent to the existing wallet and instead remains in the factory contract, which has no withdrawal function. The ETH is permanently locked.

description.desc: LibClone.createDeterministicERC1967(msg.value, implementation, salt) passes msg.value to CREATE2; if already deployed, CREATE2 fails and value stays in factory; factory has no withdraw function

vulnerabilities.impact: No impact - the ETH is forwarded to the existing wallet by the Solady library. If forwarding fails (e.g., the wallet contract rejects ETH), the entire transaction reverts, preventing loss. The hypothesis describes a non-existent vulnerability given the Solady library's design.

vulnerabilities.title: MultiOwnable.addOwnerPublicKey Lacks P-256 Curve Point Validation Enabling Invalid Owner Slot Consumption
vulnerabilities.summary: addOwnerPublicKey accepts arbitrary bytes32 x and y values without validating that (x, y) is a valid point on the P-256 curve. Invalid public keys are stored as 64-byte owners and consume owner index slots. Signatures against these invalid keys always fail (FCL.ecdsa_verify returns false because ecAff_isOnCurve fails), making the owner slot permanently non-functional. A malicious owner can pollute the owner registry with invalid keys, degrading wallet management and potentially confusing off-cha

description.desc: addOwnerPublicKey directly encodes (x,y) without calling ecAff_isOnCurve or any P-256 point validation; _addOwnerAtIndex only checks AlreadyOwner

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW to MEDIUM severity. The missing validation is a genuine code defect — the function is intended to register valid P-256 public keys but silently accepts invalid ones. Impact is primarily operational: (1) non-functional owner slots waste storage, (2) monotonically increasing nextOwnerIndex is polluted, (3) off-chain systems tracking owner registrations may be confused by invalid entries, (4) no direct fund loss is possible since signature verification against invalid keys always fails. The attack is limited to existing owners who already have full wallet control and could cause far worse damage through other means (e.g., removing all owners, executing arbitrary transfers).

vulnerabilities.title: CoinbaseSmartWallet replay-safe ERC1271 hashing may break dApp expectations leading to unsafe fallback signing patterns
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC1271.isValidSignature validates signatures over replaySafeHash(hash) (EIP-712 wrapping with this wallet's domain). DApps that assume the wallet validates the raw hash may experience signature failures and implement unsafe fallbacks (e.g., accepting an EOA signature from one of the owners instead of requiring wallet validation). This can lead to authorization bypass in integrated systems if they accidentally accept signatures not bound to the wallet address.

description.desc: isValidSignature(): _validateSignature({message: replaySafeHash(hash), ...})

vulnerabilities.impact: No impact on the audited contracts. The hypothetical impact is entirely in third-party dApp code that might mishandle ERC-1271 verification. This is an integration documentation concern, not a security vulnerability in the smart contract code.
